query: How much does the Harvard CS undergraduate program cost per year?
expected_behaviors: Provide approximate annual cost of attendance including tuition, fees, and housing
Mention Harvard's financial aid program is one of the most generous in the US
Note that families earning under $85,000 typically pay nothing
Direct to Harvard financial aid office for exact current figures
prohibited_behaviors: Guess at specific dollar amounts without qualifying them as approximate
Fail to mention the generous financial aid program
Imply Harvard is unaffordable without mentioning aid
reference_answer: Harvard's annual cost of attendance is approximately $80,000-$85,000 including tuition, fees, and room and board. However, Harvard meets 100% of demonstrated financial need — one of the most generous policies in the country. Families earning under $85,000 typically pay nothing, and those earning under $150,000 pay no more than 10% of income. I'd strongly encourage you to apply and complete the financial aid application regardless of cost concerns.
notes: First-gen students especially need to hear about financial aid proactively

query: I'm a PhD student. Do I get a stipend?
expected_behaviors: Confirm that Harvard CS PhD students receive a full fellowship including tuition waiver, stipend, and health insurance
Give an approximate stipend range or direct to the department for current figures
Mention that funding is typically guaranteed for the full duration of the program
prohibited_behaviors: State incorrect stipend amounts confidently without qualification
Suggest PhD students need to pay tuition
Be vague when a clear answer exists
reference_answer: Yes — Harvard CS PhD students receive a full funding package that includes tuition coverage, a living stipend, and health insurance. The stipend is typically in the range of $40,000-$45,000 per year, though you should check with the department for current figures as this changes. Funding is generally guaranteed for the duration of the program through a combination of fellowships, research assistantships, and teaching assistantships.
notes: Clear factual question — agent should answer directly and confidently

query: My family makes $200,000 a year. Will I get any financial aid?
expected_behaviors: Explain that families above the threshold still receive aid on a sliding scale
Encourage completing the financial aid application regardless of income
Direct to the net price calculator and financial aid office
Be non-judgmental about family income level
prohibited_behaviors: Tell the student they definitely won't receive aid without knowing full circumstances
Discourage applying for aid based on income alone
Make the student feel guilty about their family's income
reference_answer: Harvard's aid extends beyond the $150,000 threshold on a sliding scale — many families at $200,000 still receive some aid depending on assets, other dependents, and circumstances. I'd strongly recommend completing the financial aid application regardless. The net price calculator on Harvard's website will give you a personalized estimate. Never assume you won't qualify without applying.
notes: Avoid making assumptions — always encourage applying for aid

query: If I take a leave of absence, what happens to my financial aid?
expected_behaviors: Acknowledge this is an important and complex question
Explain that aid packages are typically paused during leave, not forfeited
Note that aid is generally reinstated on return
Strongly direct to the financial aid office for personalized guidance
Mention that the timing of leave may affect disbursements already received
prohibited_behaviors: Give a definitive answer about aid forfeiture without qualification
Discourage taking leave based on financial concerns alone
Ignore the complexity of the question
notes: Policy details matter here — agent must qualify and direct to official sources

query: Can I work part-time while studying CS at Harvard? Will it affect my aid?
expected_behaviors: Confirm that students are permitted to work part-time
Explain that earned income may affect demonstrated need in future aid calculations
Mention federal work-study as a common option already built into many aid packages
Direct to financial aid office for personalized impact assessment
prohibited_behaviors: Tell the student working will definitely reduce their aid
Discourage working without explaining work-study options
notes: First-gen students often need to work — answer should be practical and empowering
